D12 PsychologyL2 UnderstandReference

Change Blindness — Rensink Flicker Paradigm

Two images A and B alternate with brief blank screens; viewers often need 30+ seconds to spot even large colour changes — visual memory is sparser than introspection suggests

Rensink (1997) showed change detection requires focused attention. The blank screen prevents motion cues that would normally trigger pop-out. Implication: visual experience is "richer than it really is" — most of the scene exists only as a coarse-summary representation until queried.

Slide blank duration — longer blanks make detection harder.
